q: What is a table in SQL?
a: A table stores data in rows and columns, like a spreadsheet. Each row is one record (e.g. one customer) and each column is one field about it (e.g. name, email). Almost all SQL works by reading from or writing to tables.
fs: A table is rows (records) by columns (fields) — the grid all SQL reads from and writes to.
fj: Thinking a table is just one list of values, not a grid where each row shares the same columns.

a: SELECT reads data out of a table and shows it back to you. You list the columns you want (or * for all of them) and the table to read from with FROM. SELECT only looks at data; it never changes it.
fs: SELECT ... FROM is how you read columns out of a table — it returns rows, it doesn't modify them.
fj: Assuming SELECT changes or saves data; it only reads and returns rows.

a: WHERE filters rows so you only get the ones that match a condition. You write a rule like city = 'Austin' and SQL keeps only the rows where that rule is true. Without WHERE, you get every row.
fs: WHERE keeps only rows where its condition is true — it filters which rows come back, before any grouping.
fj: Confusing WHERE (picks rows) with SELECT (picks columns), or expecting it to pick columns.

a: A JOIN combines rows from two tables by matching a shared value, so you can see related data together. For example, match each order to its customer using customer id. The ON clause says which columns must match.
fs: A JOIN stitches two tables together on a matching key (ON), so related rows line up in one result.
fj: Thinking JOIN stacks tables on top of each other; it matches rows side by side on a shared key.

a: GROUP BY collapses many rows into one row per group, so you can summarize. You pick a column to group on (like city), then use a function like COUNT or SUM to total each group. It answers 'how many / how much per group?'.
fs: GROUP BY folds rows into one per group so aggregates like COUNT/SUM report a value per group.
fj: Expecting raw rows back; GROUP BY returns one summarized row per group, not the originals.
